content:
# James Chapter 1 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### Verse 1:1
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Iakobos, of-God and of-[the]-Lord Iesous Christou a-slave, to-the twelve tribes, the [ones] in the dispersion: To-rejoice." |
| LOGOS | "James, a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ, to the twelve tribes in the Dispersion: Greeting." |
| PNEUMA | "From James, a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ -- to the twelve tribes scattered across the world: Greetings." |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET transliterates "Iakobos," "Iesous Christou"; LOGOS and PNEUMA use anglicized forms. (2) EMET uses "slave"; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "servant." (3) EMET uses "dispersion" (lowercase); LOGOS capitalizes "Dispersion"; PNEUMA paraphrases as "scattered across the world." (4) EMET uses "To-rejoice" (literal ΧΑΙΡΕΙΝ); LOGOS uses "Greeting"; PNEUMA uses "Greetings." (5) PNEUMA adds "From" before the name.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS as base. "James" (anglicized), "servant," "Dispersion" (capitalized as a technical term), "Greeting" (singular, matching the Greek infinitive).

---

### Verse 1:2
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "All joy consider [it], my brothers, whenever you-fall-into trials various" |
| LOGOS | "Count it all joy, my brothers, when you fall into various trials" |
| PNEUMA | "My brothers and sisters, consider it pure joy when you find yourselves surrounded by trials of every kind" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "consider"; LOGOS uses "count"; PNEUMA uses "consider." (2) EMET uses "All joy" (fronted); LOGOS uses "all joy"; PNEUMA uses "pure joy." (3) PNEUMA adds "and sisters." (4) PNEUMA paraphrases "fall into" as "find yourselves surrounded by." (5) EMET uses "various"; LOGOS uses "various"; PNEUMA uses "of every kind."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "Count it all joy" follows LOGOS. "My brothers" (without "and sisters"). "Various trials" (LOGOS/EMET agreement). "When you fall into" (LOGOS).

---

### Verse 1:3
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "knowing that the testing of-your faith works-out endurance" |
| LOGOS | "knowing that the testing of your faith produces endurance" |
| PNEUMA | "because you know that when your faith is tested, it produces the ability to endure" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "works-out"; LOGOS uses "produces"; PNEUMA uses "produces." (2) PNEUMA restructures from a participial clause ("knowing that") to a causal clause ("because you know that"). (3) PNEUMA renders "endurance" as "the ability to endure." (4) PNEUMA rephrases "the testing of your faith" as "when your faith is tested."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "Knowing that the testing of your faith produces endurance."

---

### Verse 1:4
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And let-the endurance have [its] work complete, in-order-that you-may-be complete and whole, in nothing lacking" |
| LOGOS | "And let endurance have its full effect, that you may be perfect and complete, lacking in nothing" |
| PNEUMA | "But let that endurance finish its work in you, so that you may become mature and complete, not lacking anything" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "work complete"; LOGOS uses "full effect"; PNEUMA uses "finish its work." (2) EMET uses "complete and whole"; LOGOS uses "perfect and complete"; PNEUMA uses "mature and complete." (3) PNEUMA uses "But" instead of "And." (4) PNEUMA uses "so that" instead of "that/in-order-that."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS as base, substituting "complete and whole" for "perfect and complete." The Greek ΤΕΛΕΙΟΙ ΚΑΙ ΟΛΟΚΛΗΡΟΙ is better served by "complete and whole" -- using "perfect" for ΤΕΛΕΙΟΙ here risks confusion with the moral sense when the meaning is "brought to completion." "Lacking in nothing" from LOGOS.

---

### Verse 1:5
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "But if any of-you lacks wisdom, let-him-ask from the [one] giving -- God -- to-all generously and not reproaching, and it-will-be-given to-him" |
| LOGOS | "But if any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask of God, who gives to all generously and without reproach, and it shall be given to him" |
| PNEUMA | "If any of you lacks wisdom, ask God for it -- the One who gives generously to everyone without finding fault -- and it will be given to you" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET and LOGOS include "But"; PNEUMA omits it. (2) EMET uses "not reproaching"; LOGOS uses "without reproach"; PNEUMA uses "without finding fault." (3) PNEUMA shifts to second person ("you") throughout. (4) PNEUMA adds "for it" after "ask God."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. Retains "But" (the Greek ΔΕ is present). "Without reproach" is preferable to PNEUMA's paraphrase.

---

### Verse 1:6
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "But let-him-ask in faith, nothing doubting; for the [one] doubting is-like a-wave of-[the]-sea, being-driven-by-wind and being-tossed" |
| LOGOS | "But let him ask in faith, with no doubting, for the one who doubts is like a wave of the sea, driven and tossed by the wind" |
| PNEUMA | "But you must ask with confidence, without doubting, because the person who doubts is like a wave of the sea, blown and tossed by the wind" |

**Divergences:** (1) PNEUMA uses "with confidence" instead of "in faith." (2) EMET uses "nothing doubting"; LOGOS uses "with no doubting"; PNEUMA uses "without doubting." (3) PNEUMA uses "blown" instead of "driven." (4) PNEUMA uses "because" instead of "for."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "In faith" preserves the Greek ΕΝ ΠΙΣΤΕΙ. "Driven and tossed by the wind" (LOGOS).

---

### Verse 1:7
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "For let- not -suppose that man that he-will-receive anything from the Lord" |
| LOGOS | "For let not that man suppose that he shall receive anything from the Lord --" |
| PNEUMA | "That person should not expect to receive anything from the Lord --" |

**Divergences:** (1) PNEUMA uses "person" instead of "man." (2) PNEUMA uses "expect" instead of "suppose." (3) PNEUMA omits "For." (4) LOGOS uses "shall"; EMET uses "will"; PNEUMA restructures.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "For let not that man suppose that he shall receive anything from the Lord."

---

### Verse 1:8
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "[he is] a-double-souled man, unstable in all his ways" |
| LOGOS | "a double-minded man, unstable in all his ways" |
| PNEUMA | "they are divided in their loyalties, unstable in everything they do" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "double-souled"; LOGOS uses "double-minded"; PNEUMA uses "divided in their loyalties." (2) PNEUMA uses "everything they do" instead of "all his ways." (3) PNEUMA uses plural "they" to avoid gender specification.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "Double-minded" is the traditional calque of ΔΙΨΥΧΟΣ. "Unstable in all his ways."

---

### Verse 1:9
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "But let-boast the brother, the lowly [one], in his exaltation" |
| LOGOS | "Let the brother of low position boast in his exaltation" |
| PNEUMA | "Believers who are poor should take pride in their high standing" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET includes "But"; LOGOS and PNEUMA omit it. (2) EMET uses "the lowly [one]"; LOGOS uses "of low position"; PNEUMA uses "who are poor." (3) PNEUMA uses "Believers" instead of "the brother." (4) PNEUMA uses "take pride" instead of "boast." (5) PNEUMA uses "high standing" instead of "exaltation."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "Let the brother of low position boast in his exaltation." The Greek ΤΑΠΕΙΝΟΣ means "lowly/humble" more than specifically "poor."

---

### Verse 1:10
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and the rich [one] in his humiliation, because as a-flower of-grass he-will-pass-away" |
| LOGOS | "but the rich man in his humiliation, because like a flower of the grass he shall pass away" |
| PNEUMA | "and those who are rich should take pride in their low standing, because they will fade away like a wildflower" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "and"; LOGOS uses "but"; PNEUMA uses "and." (2) EMET uses "as"; LOGOS uses "like"; PNEUMA uses "like." (3) PNEUMA uses "wildflower" instead of "flower of the grass." (4) PNEUMA uses "fade away" instead of "pass away." (5) PNEUMA uses "low standing" instead of "humiliation."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS as base, but use "and" instead of "but" (the Greek ΔΕ here continues the parallel). "Like a flower of the grass he shall pass away."

---

### Verse 1:11
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "For the sun rose with the scorching-heat and withered the grass, and the flower of-it fell-off, and the beauty of-the face of-it perished; so also the rich [one] in his goings will-be-withered" |
| LOGOS | "For the sun rises with its scorching heat and withers the grass, and its flower falls, and the beauty of its appearance perishes. So also shall the rich man fade away in his pursuits" |
| PNEUMA | "For the sun rises with its scorching heat and withers the plant; its blossom falls and its beauty is destroyed. In the same way, the wealthy will wither away even in the midst of their pursuits" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses aorist past tenses ("rose," "withered," "fell-off," "perished"); LOGOS and PNEUMA use present tense. (2) EMET uses "flower of-it fell-off"; LOGOS uses "its flower falls"; PNEUMA uses "its blossom falls." (3) EMET uses "face of-it"; LOGOS uses "appearance"; PNEUMA omits. (4) EMET uses "goings"; LOGOS uses "pursuits"; PNEUMA uses "pursuits." (5) PNEUMA uses "plant" instead of "grass." (6) PNEUMA uses "the wealthy" instead of "the rich man/one."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. The gnomic aorists are best rendered as English present tenses. "Pursuits" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for ΠΟΡΕΙΑΙΣ.

---

### Verse 1:12
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Blessed [is the] man who endures trial, because having-become approved he-will-receive the crown of-the life which he-promised to-the [ones] loving him" |
| LOGOS | "Blessed is the man who endures trial, for when he has been approved he shall receive the crown of life, which the Lord has promised to those who love him" |
| PNEUMA | "Blessed is the person who stands firm under trial, because after enduring the test, that person will receive the crown of life that God has promised to those who love him" |

**Divergences:** (1) PNEUMA uses "person" instead of "man." (2) EMET uses "endures trial"; LOGOS uses "endures trial"; PNEUMA uses "stands firm under trial." (3) EMET has "he promised" (ambiguous subject); LOGOS supplies "the Lord"; PNEUMA supplies "God." (4) EMET uses "loving him"; LOGOS uses "who love him"; PNEUMA uses "who love him."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS as base with one correction: Sinaiticus reads ΕΠΗΓΓΕΙΛΑΤΟ without an explicit subject. EMET correctly preserves the ambiguity with "he promised." SOPHIA renders "he has promised" -- retaining the manuscript's ambiguity while using the English perfect tense.

---

### Verse 1:13
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Let- no-one being-tempted -say that 'From God I-am-being-tempted'; for God is untemptable by-evils, and he himself tempts no-one" |
| LOGOS | "Let no one being tempted say, 'I am being tempted by God,' for God is not tempted by evil, and he himself tempts no one" |
| PNEUMA | "When someone is tempted, they should never say, 'God is tempting me,' for God cannot be tempted by evil, and he himself tempts no one" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "untemptable by-evils" (plural); LOGOS uses "not tempted by evil"; PNEUMA uses "cannot be tempted by evil." (2) EMET preserves "From God" word order; LOGOS and PNEUMA restructure. (3) PNEUMA restructures "Let no one... say" as "they should never say." (4) PNEUMA uses "God is tempting me" instead of "I am being tempted by God."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS, with "cannot be tempted by evil" (taking ΑΠΕΙΡΑΣΤΟΣ as "unable to be tempted," supported by both EMET's "untemptable" and PNEUMA's "cannot be tempted").

---

### Verse 1:14
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "But each [one] is-tempted by his-own desire, being-dragged-out and being-enticed" |
| LOGOS | "But each one is tempted when he is drawn away and enticed by his own desire" |
| PNEUMA | "Rather, each person is tempted when they are dragged away and lured by their own desire" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "being-dragged-out"; LOGOS uses "drawn away"; PNEUMA uses "dragged away." (2) PNEUMA uses "lured" instead of "enticed." (3) PNEUMA uses "Rather" instead of "But." (4) PNEUMA uses "person" and "they/their."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "Drawn away and enticed" balances accuracy and readability.

---

### Verse 1:15
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Then the desire, having-conceived, gives-birth-to sin; and the sin, having-been-completed, brings-forth death" |
| LOGOS | "Then desire, having conceived, gives birth to sin; and sin, when it is fully grown, brings forth death" |
| PNEUMA | "Then desire conceives and gives birth to sin, and sin, when it has fully grown, gives birth to death" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET includes articles ("the desire," "the sin"); LOGOS and PNEUMA omit them. (2) EMET uses "having-been-completed"; LOGOS uses "when it is fully grown"; PNEUMA uses "when it has fully grown." (3) PNEUMA uses "gives birth to death" instead of "brings forth death."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "Brings forth death" for ΑΠΟΚΥΕΙ -- using a different verb than "gives birth" to distinguish ΤΙΚΤΕΙ from ΑΠΟΚΥΕΙ, as LOGOS and EMET both do.

---

### Verse 1:16
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Do- not -be-deceived, my beloved brothers" |
| LOGOS | "Do not be led astray, my beloved brothers" |
| PNEUMA | "Do not be deceived, my dear brothers and sisters" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "be-deceived"; LOGOS uses "be led astray"; PNEUMA uses "be deceived." (2) PNEUMA uses "dear" instead of "beloved." (3) PNEUMA adds "and sisters."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "Do not be deceived" (EMET/PNEUMA majority). "My beloved brothers" (EMET/LOGOS majority).

---

### Verse 1:17
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Every giving good and every gift complete is from-above, coming-down from the Father of-the lights, with whom there-is no variation or of-turning shadow" |
| LOGOS | "Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights, with whom there is no variation or shadow of turning" |
| PNEUMA | "Every good gift and every perfect present comes down from above -- from the Father of lights, with whom there is no shifting shadow or hint of change" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET preserves the Greek word order "giving good... gift complete"; LOGOS and PNEUMA restructure to English adjective-noun order. (2) EMET uses "giving" for ΔΟΣΙΣ; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "gift." (3) PNEUMA uses "present" for ΔΩΡΗΜΑ. (4) EMET uses "of-turning shadow"; LOGOS uses "shadow of turning"; PNEUMA uses "shifting shadow or hint of change." (5) PNEUMA uses "comes down" (singular) instead of "is from above, coming down."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "Shadow of turning" preserves the astronomical metaphor formally.

---

### Verse 1:18
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Having-willed, he-brought- us -forth by-a-word of-truth, for us to-be a-certain first-fruit of-his creatures" |
| LOGOS | "Having willed it, he brought us forth by the word of truth, that we should be a kind of firstfruits of his creatures" |
| PNEUMA | "He chose to give us life through the word of truth, so that we would be a kind of firstfruits among all he has created" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "Having-willed"; LOGOS uses "Having willed it"; PNEUMA uses "He chose." (2) EMET uses "brought forth"; LOGOS uses "brought forth"; PNEUMA uses "give us life." (3) EMET uses "by-a-word"; LOGOS uses "by the word"; PNEUMA uses "through the word." (4) EMET uses "a-certain first-fruit"; LOGOS uses "a kind of firstfruits"; PNEUMA uses "a kind of firstfruits." (5) PNEUMA uses "among all he has created" instead of "of his creatures."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "Brought us forth by the word of truth" preserves ΑΠΕΚΥΗΣΕΝ. "A kind of firstfruits of his creatures."

---

### Verse 1:19
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Know [this], my beloved brothers: but let- every man -be quick to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath" |
| LOGOS | "Know this, my beloved brothers: let every man be swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath" |
| PNEUMA | "Understand this, my dear brothers and sisters: everyone should be quick to listen, slow to speak, and slow to become angry" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "quick"; LOGOS uses "swift"; PNEUMA uses "quick." (2) PNEUMA uses "listen" instead of "hear." (3) PNEUMA uses "become angry" instead of "wrath." (4) PNEUMA uses "Understand" instead of "Know." (5) PNEUMA adds "and sisters."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS as base, but use "quick" (EMET/PNEUMA majority) over "swift."

---

### Verse 1:20
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "for [the] wrath of-a-man [the] righteousness of-God does- not -work" |
| LOGOS | "for the wrath of man does not produce the righteousness of God" |
| PNEUMA | "because human anger does not produce the righteousness that God desires" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "work"; LOGOS uses "produce"; PNEUMA uses "produce." (2) EMET preserves the fronted word order; LOGOS and PNEUMA restructure. (3) PNEUMA uses "human anger" instead of "wrath of man." (4) PNEUMA adds "that God desires" (interpretive expansion of "of God").

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "The wrath of man does not produce the righteousness of God."

---

### Verse 1:21
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Therefore, having-put-away all filthiness and abundance of-evil, in gentleness receive the implanted word, the [one] being-able to-save your souls" |
| LOGOS | "Therefore, putting away all filthiness and abundance of wickedness, receive with meekness the implanted word, which is able to save your souls" |
| PNEUMA | "So strip away every trace of moral filth and the evil that overflows among you, and humbly receive the word planted deep within you -- the word that has the power to save your lives" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "evil"; LOGOS uses "wickedness"; PNEUMA uses "evil that overflows." (2) EMET uses "gentleness"; LOGOS uses "meekness"; PNEUMA uses "humbly." (3) PNEUMA uses "strip away" instead of "putting away." (4) PNEUMA expands "implanted word" to "the word planted deep within you." (5) PNEUMA uses "lives" instead of "souls."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "Meekness" for ΠΡΑΥΤΗΤΙ. "Wickedness" for ΚΑΚΙΑΣ. "Which is able to save your souls."

---

### Verse 1:22
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "But become doers of-[the]-word and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves" |
| LOGOS | "But be doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves" |
| PNEUMA | "Do not merely listen to the word and fool yourselves -- put it into practice" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "become"; LOGOS uses "be." (2) PNEUMA restructures entirely, using "fool yourselves" instead of "deceiving yourselves" and "put it into practice" instead of "doers of the word."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "Be doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves."

---

### Verse 1:23
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Because if anyone is a-hearer of-[the]-word and not a-doer, this-one is-like a-man observing the face of-his origin in a-mirror" |
| LOGOS | "For if anyone is a hearer of the word and not a doer, he is like a man observing his natural face in a mirror" |
| PNEUMA | "For anyone who listens to the word but does not act on it is like someone who looks at their own face in a mirror" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "Because"; LOGOS uses "For"; PNEUMA uses "For." (2) EMET uses "the face of-his origin"; LOGOS uses "his natural face"; PNEUMA uses "their own face." (3) PNEUMA restructures from conditional to relative clause. (4) PNEUMA uses "looks at" instead of "observing."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "His natural face" is a good rendering of ΤΟ ΠΡΟΣΩΠΟΝ ΤΗΣ ΓΕΝΕΣΕΩΣ ΑΥΤΟΥ.

---

### Verse 1:24
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "for he-observed himself and has-gone-away and immediately forgot what-sort [of person] he-was" |
| LOGOS | "for he observes himself and goes away, and immediately forgets what sort of man he was" |
| PNEUMA | "takes a look, walks away, and immediately forgets what they look like" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses mixed past tenses; LOGOS uses present. (2) PNEUMA uses informal phrasing ("takes a look," "walks away"). (3) PNEUMA uses "what they look like" instead of "what sort of man he was."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. Present tenses serve the proverbial illustration well.

---

### Verse 1:25
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "But the [one] having-looked-into [the] perfect law, the [law] of-the freedom, and having-remained-beside [it], not having-become a-hearer of-forgetfulness but a-doer of-work -- this-one will-be blessed in his doing" |
| LOGOS | "But the one who looks into the perfect law, the law of liberty, and perseveres, being not a forgetful hearer but a doer of the work -- this man shall be blessed in his doing" |
| PNEUMA | "But whoever looks intently into the perfect law that sets people free and stays with it -- not forgetting what they have heard but actually doing it -- that person will be blessed in everything they do" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "freedom"; LOGOS uses "liberty"; PNEUMA paraphrases "that sets people free." (2) EMET uses "having-remained-beside"; LOGOS uses "perseveres"; PNEUMA uses "stays with it." (3) EMET uses "hearer of-forgetfulness"; LOGOS uses "forgetful hearer"; PNEUMA uses "not forgetting what they have heard." (4) PNEUMA uses "everything they do" instead of "his doing."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "The law of liberty" for ΤΟΝ ΤΗΣ ΕΛΕΥΘΕΡΙΑΣ. "Perseveres" for ΠΑΡΑΜΕΙΝΑΣ.

---

### Verse 1:26
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "If anyone considers [himself] to-be religious, not bridling his tongue but deceiving his heart, the religion of-this-one [is] worthless" |
| LOGOS | "If anyone considers himself to be religious and does not bridle his tongue but deceives his own heart, this man's religion is worthless" |
| PNEUMA | "If anyone considers themselves religious yet does not keep a tight rein on their tongue, they are deceiving their own heart, and their religion is worthless" |

**Divergences:** (1) PNEUMA uses "keep a tight rein on" instead of "bridle." (2) PNEUMA uses "yet" instead of "and... not." (3) PNEUMA uses "they/their" throughout. (4) LOGOS adds "own" before "heart."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS. "Bridle" for ΧΑΛΙΝΑΓΩΓΩΝ preserves the vivid metaphor.

---

### Verse 1:27
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Religion pure and undefiled before the God and Father is this: to-visit orphans and widows in their affliction, [and] to-keep oneself unstained from the world" |
| LOGOS | "Pure and undefiled religion before our God and Father is this: to visit orphans and widows in their affliction, and to keep oneself unstained from the world" |
| PNEUMA | "The religion that God our Father considers pure and genuine is this: to look after orphans and widows in their distress, and to keep yourself from being corrupted by the world" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "the God and Father"; LOGOS uses "our God and Father"; PNEUMA uses "God our Father." (2) EMET places "pure and undefiled" after "Religion"; LOGOS fronts them. (3) PNEUMA uses "genuine" instead of "undefiled." (4) PNEUMA uses "look after" instead of "visit." (5) PNEUMA uses "distress" instead of "affliction." (6) PNEUMA uses "corrupted by" instead of "unstained from."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS as base, but render "before God the Father" -- Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΡΑ ΤΩ ΘΕΩ ΚΑΙ ΠΑΤΡΙ without a possessive pronoun. "Unstained from the world" (EMET/LOGOS agreement).

---

**Summary:** All 27 verses of James Chapter 1 required adjudication. Every verse exhibited divergences among the three translators, as expected given the literal, formal, and dynamic methodologies. LOGOS served as the primary base text in 25 of 27 verses. EMET influenced the consensus in verses 1:4 (word choice), 1:10 (conjunction), 1:16 (verb choice), and 1:19 (adverb). PNEUMA's influence was primarily confirmatory, supporting choices where it agreed with one of the other two translators (e.g., v.13 "cannot be tempted"). The most significant adjudication decisions concerned: (a) the ambiguous subject of ΕΠΗΓΓΕΙΛΑΤΟ in v.12 (resolved by preserving ambiguity); (b) "double-minded" vs. "double-souled" vs. "divided in loyalties" in v.8; and (c) the rendering of ΤΕΛΕΙΟΙ ΚΑΙ ΟΛΟΚΛΗΡΟΙ in v.4 as "complete and whole" rather than "perfect and complete."
